In this paper, for the minimization of the weighted trace of the mean squared error among locally unbiased estimators, we prove that it suffices to consider POVMs with at most $\left({\rm dim}\,\mathcal{H}\right)^{2}+d(d+1)/2-1$ outcomes, and that an optimal measurement can be chosen to be rank-one. For the minimization of the average weighted trace of the mean squared error in Bayesian estimation, we show that it suffices to consider POVMs with at most $\left( {\rm dim}\, \mathcal{H}\right)^{2}$outcomes, and again an optimal POVM can be taken to be rank-one. Furthermore, when the model admits a real sufficient subalgebra, we show that the $\left( {\rm dim}\, \mathcal{H} \right)^{2}$ term in the above support-size bounds can be reduced in both the locally unbiased and Bayesian settings. These bounds substantially reduce the search space for optimal measurements and justify restricting numerical optimization to rank-one POVMs with finitely many outcomes.

AI.tldr: This paper proves that for quantum estimation problems, optimal POVMs can be restricted to rank-one measurements with at most (dim H)² + d(d+1)/2 - 1 outcomes for locally unbiased estimation and (dim H)² outcomes for Bayesian estimation, with further reductions possible when a real sufficient subalgebra exists.
AI.motivation: The search for optimal estimators in quantum estimation is computationally prohibitive because the number of POVM outcomes is unbounded, making the space of admissible measurements vast. The paper aims to establish finite upper bounds on the necessary number of outcomes and to show that rank-one measurements suffice, thereby reducing the search space.
AI.method: The authors employ convex optimization and duality theory, specifically using Carathéodory's theorem on convex hulls, to derive support-size bounds for POVMs in both locally unbiased and Bayesian estimation frameworks. They also leverage the concept of real sufficient subalgebras to tighten these bounds.
AI.result: - For locally unbiased estimation (minimizing weighted trace of MSE): suffices to consider rank-one POVMs with at most (dim H)² + d(d+1)/2 - 1 outcomes. - For Bayesian estimation (minimizing average weighted trace of MSE): suffices to consider rank-one POVMs with at most (dim H)² outcomes. - If the model has a real sufficient subalgebra, the (dim H)² term can be reduced in both settings.
AI.conclusion: These bounds significantly shrink the search space for optimal quantum measurements, justifying the restriction to rank-one POVMs with finitely many outcomes in numerical optimization, and provide theoretical grounding for practical algorithm design in quantum metrology.

title: pygridsynth: A fast numerical tool for ancilla-free Clifford+T synthesis
summary: We present pygridsynth, an open-source Python library for ancilla-free approximate Clifford+$T$ synthesis that runs in $O(\log(1/ε))$ for precision $ε$. For $n=1, 2$ qubits, the library builds upon established efficient and high-precision synthesis routines, such as nearly optimal $Z$-rotation synthesis and magnitude approximation. For $n\ge 3$ qubits, we introduce a partial-decomposition technique that generalizes the magnitude approximation, reducing constant factors in the $T$-count as $(\frac{21}{8}\cdot 4^n - \frac{9}{2}\cdot 2^n + 9)\log_2(1/ε) + o(\log(1/ε))$. The package also exposes a mixed-synthesis workflow that approximates target unitary channels by probabilistic mixtures of Clifford+$T$ circuits, for which we empirically find that the synthesis error is reduced from $ε$ to $ε^2/(2n)$. Taken together, these features make pygridsynth a Python-native platform for high-precision Clifford$+T$ synthesis and for benchmarking unitary and mixed synthesis strategies on multi-qubit instances.

AI.tldr: pygridsynth is an open-source Python library for ancilla-free approximate Clifford+T synthesis with O(log(1/ε)) runtime, introducing a partial-decomposition technique for n≥3 qubits and a mixed-synthesis workflow that reduces error from ε to ε²/(2n).
AI.motivation: To provide a Python-native, open-source platform for high-precision Clifford+T synthesis that generalizes existing single- and two-qubit routines to multi-qubit instances while reducing T-count constant factors and supporting mixed-synthesis strategies.
AI.method: The library builds on established Z-rotation synthesis and magnitude approximation for n=1,2 qubits. For n≥3 qubits, it introduces a partial-decomposition technique that generalizes magnitude approximation, achieving T-count of (21/8·4^n - 9/2·2^n + 9)log₂(1/ε) + o(log(1/ε)). It also exposes a mixed-synthesis workflow using probabilistic mixtures of Clifford+T circuits.
AI.result: The partial-decomposition technique reduces constant factors in T-count for n≥3 qubits. Empirically, the mixed-synthesis workflow reduces synthesis error from ε to ε²/(2n).
AI.conclusion: pygridsynth enables high-precision ancilla-free Clifford+T synthesis and benchmarking of both unitary and mixed synthesis strategies on multi-qubit instances within a Python-native framework.

title: Scalable surface ion trap design for magnetic quantum sensing and gradiometry
summary: Magnetic quantum sensors based on trapped ions utilize properties of quantum mechanics which have optimized precision and beat current limits in sensor technology. Trapped ions are highly sensitive in a large span of signal ranging from DC or static B-field to the radiofrequency range in 100s of MHz and can attain the sensitivity in the range of pT to sub pT . They are tuneable to frequencies of interest and can be used as a lock-in frequency detector. This modelling and simulation based study presents an innovative design of Surface Paul Traps, enabling the use of trapped ions as ultra-sensitive sensors for magnetic field detection and precise measurement of magnetic field gradients at a sub-millimeter spatial resolution. The novel design features multiple trapping regions, allowing for the mapping of magnetic fields across various ion-trapping zones. The study demonstrates groundbreaking advancements in ion manipulation and confinement through innovative chip architecture.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an innovative Surface Paul Trap design for trapped-ion-based magnetic quantum sensors, featuring multiple trapping regions for ultra-sensitive magnetic field detection and gradient measurement at sub-millimeter spatial resolution, with tunable sensitivity from pT to sub-pT levels across DC to RF frequencies.
AI.motivation: To overcome current limits in sensor technology by developing trapped-ion-based magnetic quantum sensors with optimized precision, tunability, and high spatial resolution for magnetic field detection.
AI.method: Modeling and simulation-based study of a novel Surface Paul Trap architecture with multiple trapping regions, enabling ion manipulation, confinement, and magnetic field mapping across different zones.
AI.result: The design achieves sub-millimeter spatial resolution, sensitivity ranging from pT to sub-pT, and operates across DC to hundreds of MHz frequency range with lock-in detection capability.
AI.conclusion: The innovative Surface Paul Trap design with multiple trapping regions represents a groundbreaking advancement in trapped-ion technology for ultra-sensitive magnetic field sensing and gradient measurement.

title: Ghost Degrees of Freedom Without Quantum Runaway: Exact Moment Bounds from an Operator Conservation Law
summary: We prove an exact quantum conservation law for a harmonic oscillator coupled to a ghost degree of freedom: a second classical conserved quantity lifts to a quantum operator that commutes with the Hamiltonian with no hbar corrections, yielding a rigorous, state-independent upper bound on the mean squared phase-space radius for all time and every quantum state with finite initial second moments. The proof uses only canonical commutation relations and the Leibniz rule; it requires no confining potential, no spectral assumptions, and no perturbative expansion. The interaction studied here is bounded and vanishes at large separations, the generic situation in effective field theory, yet this suffices to guarantee quantum stability in the sense of bounded second moments. Three independent numerical frameworks (Heisenberg picture, Schrodinger picture, and Fock-space diagonalization) confirm wavepacket confinement below the analytic bound, a real energy spectrum, and Poisson level statistics numerically consistent with an integrable structure. The absence of a confining potential means the proof is silent on spectral discreteness and the existence of a ground state; those questions, addressed for polynomial confining interactions in concurrent work, remain open for the interaction class studied here and represent the sharpest targets for future work. Ghost quantum instability is therefore not an inevitable consequence of a wrong-sign kinetic term but depends critically on the interaction structure.

AI.tldr: A harmonic oscillator coupled to a ghost degree of freedom is shown to have an exact quantum conservation law that bounds the mean squared phase-space radius for all time, proving quantum stability despite the wrong-sign kinetic term, provided the interaction is bounded and short-ranged.
AI.motivation: Ghost degrees of freedom (wrong-sign kinetic terms) are typically thought to cause quantum instability. This work challenges that assumption by asking whether a specific interaction structure can yield stability without confining potentials or spectral assumptions.
AI.method: The proof uses only canonical commutation relations and the Leibniz rule, with no confining potential, spectral assumptions, or perturbative expansion. Numerical confirmation employs three independent frameworks: Heisenberg picture, Schrödinger picture, and Fock-space diagonalization.
AI.result: An exact quantum conservation law yields a state-independent upper bound on the mean squared phase-space radius for all time. Numerical results confirm wavepacket confinement below the analytic bound, a real energy spectrum, and Poisson level statistics consistent with integrability.
AI.conclusion: Ghost quantum instability is not inevitable from a wrong-sign kinetic term; it depends critically on the interaction structure. The proof is silent on spectral discreteness and ground state existence, which remain open for this interaction class.

title: Vertical Shuttling Protocols for Trapped Ions in Multi-Rail, Multi-Zone Surface Ion Trap Architectures
summary: We investigate optimized vertical ion-shuttling protocols for trapped-ion applications across a range of ion-trap experiments, including three-dimensional gradient-measurement sensors, on-chip ion fluorescence collection and imaging, improved laser accessibility, and quantum information processing. In this work, we focus on minimizing motional energy gain during ion transport. Our findings indicate that anomalous heating becomes the dominant limiting factor only for shuttling durations exceeding $500~μ\mathrm{s}$, whereas the final motional excitation is strongly dependent on the selected transport protocol. Using a recently measured heating rate of $(3.1 \pm 0.35)$ quanta/ms at an ion--surface separation of $134 \pm 1.5~μ\mathrm{m}$, we demonstrate that the motional excitation can be restricted to fewer than eight quanta when the ion is vertically displaced by $86~μ\mathrm{m}$ from its initial position. These results enable adiabatic shuttling within $0.5~\mathrm{ms}$, thereby meeting the operational requirements for high-fidelity quantum sensing and coherent control.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates optimized vertical ion-shuttling protocols for trapped-ion applications, showing that with proper protocol selection, motional excitation can be limited to fewer than 8 quanta for an 86 μm vertical displacement within 0.5 ms, enabling high-fidelity quantum sensing and coherent control.
AI.motivation: To develop optimized vertical ion-shuttling protocols that minimize motional energy gain during transport for various trapped-ion applications including 3D gradient-measurement sensors, on-chip fluorescence collection, improved laser accessibility, and quantum information processing.
AI.method: The authors analyze anomalous heating as a limiting factor, finding it dominates only for shuttling durations >500 μs. They use measured heating rates (3.1 ± 0.35 quanta/ms at 134 ± 1.5 μm ion-surface separation) and compare different transport protocols to minimize final motional excitation during vertical shuttling.
AI.result: Motional excitation can be restricted to fewer than 8 quanta when the ion is vertically displaced by 86 μm; adiabatic shuttling is achievable within 0.5 ms, with anomalous heating becoming dominant only beyond 500 μs.
AI.conclusion: Optimized vertical shuttling protocols meet operational requirements for high-fidelity quantum sensing and coherent control, with final motional excitation strongly dependent on protocol selection rather than anomalous heating for practical shuttling timescales.

title: Bayesian Phase Stabilization at the Shot-Noise Limit for Scalable Quantum Networks
summary: High-precision optical phase stabilization in quantum networks is fundamentally constrained by the strict photon-flux and duty-cycle limits required to avoid disturbing fragile quantum states. This challenge becomes especially critical when coordinating multiple independent light sources for multi-step quantum protocols. Here, we develop an integrated phase-stabilization framework that incorporates a Bayesian phase estimator to optimally extract information from sparse single-photon detection events. This approach outperforms conventional maximum-likelihood estimation and achieves the shot-noise limit under minimal photon flux. The framework enables real-time correction of combined phase noise from both nodal lasers and transmission fibers, facilitating a two-step excitation protocol for heralded entanglement generation between separate trapped-ion nodes via single-photon interference. Operating with a detected photon rate of approximately 1 MHz and a duty cycle less than or equal to 6.5%, the system maintains interferometric visibility greater than 97% over fiber links of 10 km and 100 km. This phase control yields deterministic ion-ion entanglement with parity contrast exceeding 85% at both distances, enabling device-independent quantum key distribution. Moreover, the resulting memory-memory entanglement at 10 km survives beyond the average time required to establish it -- a fundamental requirement for quantum repeaters. This work establishes a robust and scalable foundation for practical long-distance quantum networks.

AI.tldr: A Bayesian phase-estimation framework using sparse single-photon detection achieves shot-noise-limited phase stabilization for quantum networks, enabling high-visibility (>97%) interferometry over 100 km fiber links and deterministic ion-ion entanglement with >85% parity contrast, meeting key requirements for quantum repeaters.
AI.motivation: High-precision optical phase stabilization in quantum networks is constrained by strict photon-flux and duty-cycle limits needed to avoid disturbing fragile quantum states, especially when coordinating multiple independent light sources for multi-step quantum protocols.
AI.method: Developed an integrated phase-stabilization framework using a Bayesian phase estimator to optimally extract information from sparse single-photon detection events, enabling real-time correction of combined phase noise from nodal lasers and transmission fibers in a two-step excitation protocol for heralded entanglement generation between trapped-ion nodes.
AI.result: Achieved >97% interferometric visibility over 10 km and 100 km fiber links with ~1 MHz detected photon rate and ≤6.5% duty cycle; deterministic ion-ion entanglement with >85% parity contrast at both distances; memory-memory entanglement at 10 km outlasted the average establishment time.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes a robust and scalable foundation for practical long-distance quantum networks, with demonstrated compatibility with device-independent quantum key distribution and quantum repeater requirements.

title: HEOM-in-Calibration-Loop: Exposing Non-Markovian Bath Signatures That Markovian Calibration Elides in Superconducting-Qubit Tune-Up
comment: 6 pages + 1 page reference, 3 figures. Comments/suggestions are welcome
summary: Closed-loop superconducting-qubit calibration has matured into DAG-orchestrated protocol chains, yet published frameworks treat the bath via a Markovian master equation or a phenomenological likelihood, absorbing bath structure into fit residuals instead of reporting it as a diagnostic. We integrate a QuTiP 5.x hierarchical-equations-of-motion (HEOM) solver driven by a Tier-1 1/f Burkard bath into a multi-protocol calibration DAG (Rabi -> {Ramsey || T1}) and benchmark it against sesolve and mesolve on a frozen platform in a pulse-level simulator (no hardware validation). The Ramsey channel carries the headline: the Markovian fit is censored by its exponential-family numerical ceiling, while HEOM recovers a physical revival envelope whose primary T2* separates from the Markovian reference by at least 13x at 95% independent-bootstrap confidence within the HEOM-feasible budget; the point-estimate ratio reaches >=28x on the 50-point primary-t1 grid and ~72x on the 30-point biexp-family tau_aw pivot at L=5. Rabi contrast falls 2.17% below mesolve on a noise-limited 30-point grid; the paired-bootstrap CI crosses zero, so this channel corroborates rather than independently establishes the non-Markovian signature. T1 decay shape matches across backends (beta=1.000), yet HEOM's initial occupation drops from 1.000 to 0.879 -- a bath-dressed contamination stable under a 16-point densification. The DAG adds 9.62 us average per-protocol scheduling overhead, no meaningful latency penalty at protocol granularity. HEOM-in-loop thereby changes what calibration reports: bath structure appears as a quantifiable residual rather than a hidden confound.

AI.tldr: This paper integrates a QuTiP 5.x HEOM solver with a 1/f Burkard bath model into a closed-loop multi-protocol calibration DAG (Rabi → {Ramsey || T1}) for superconducting qubits, replacing the traditional Markovian master equation. Benchmarking against sesolve/mesolve on a pulse-level simulator shows that HEOM captures non-Markovian revival envelopes in Ramsey measurements (T2* separated by ≥13x at 95% CI), while Markovian fits fail due to exponential-family numerical ceilings. Rabi results corroborate the non-Markovian signature (2.17% contrast difference, CI crossing zero). T1 decay shape matches across backends, but HEOM reveals a bath-dressed initial occupation drop (1.000 → 0.879). The scheduling overhead is minimal (9.62 µs per protocol). The key contribution is that HEOM-in-loop makes bath structure a quantifiable residual rather than a hidden confound.
AI.motivation: Existing closed-loop calibration frameworks for superconducting qubits treat the bath using Markovian master equations or phenomenological likelihoods, absorbing bath-induced structure into fit residuals rather than reporting it as a diagnostic. This work aims to make bath structure explicit and quantifiable within the calibration loop.
AI.method: Integrate a QuTiP 5.x hierarchical-equations-of-motion (HEOM) solver driven by a Tier-1 1/f Burkard bath into a multi-protocol calibration DAG (Rabi → {Ramsey || T1}). Benchmark against sesolve and mesolve on a frozen platform in a pulse-level simulator (no hardware validation). Use independent bootstrap confidence intervals and point-estimate ratios to quantify differences.
AI.result: Ramsey: HEOM recovers a physical revival envelope; T2* separates from Markovian reference by ≥13x at 95% CI (point-estimate ratio ≥28x on 50-point t1 grid, ~72x on 30-point biexp tau_aw at L=5). Rabi: contrast 2.17% below mesolve, paired-bootstrap CI crosses zero (corroborates but does not independently establish non-Markovian signature). T1: decay shape matches (beta=1.000), but HEOM initial occupation drops from 1.000 to 0.879 (bath-dressed contamination). DAG overhead: 9.62 µs per protocol.
AI.conclusion: HEOM-in-loop changes what calibration reports: bath structure appears as a quantifiable residual rather than a hidden confound. The approach is feasible with minimal latency overhead, enabling non-Markovian diagnostics in routine calibration.

title: Dynamical Regimes of Two Qubits Coupled through a Transmission Line
summary: We investigate the reduced dynamics of two identical superconducting qubits capacitively coupled through a finite-length transmission line. Starting from circuit quantization, we derive a circuit Hamiltonian that naturally separates the line modes into even- and odd-parity sectors coupled to collective qubit operators. Depending on the hierarchy between the qubit frequency $ω_q$, the mode spacing $ω_{TL}$, and the coupling scale $ω_g$, the line acts either as a structured reservoir or as a discrete few-mode coupler. In the long-line continuum limit, each sector is described by a Drude--Lorentz spectral density and the dynamics is solved with the hierarchical equations of motion. Using the Breuer--Laine--Piilo measure, we identify the parameter region in which the reduced dynamics exhibits non-Markovian relaxation. In the short-line limit, the continuum description breaks down and the dynamics becomes respectively multimode or single-mode. This establishes a unified cQED picture of the dynamical regimes of finite-length transmission lines in superconducting-circuit architectures.

AI.tldr: The paper studies two identical superconducting qubits coupled via a finite transmission line, deriving a circuit Hamiltonian with even/odd parity sectors. Depending on line length relative to qubit frequency and coupling, the line behaves as a structured reservoir (continuum) or discrete coupler. Non-Markovian dynamics are identified using the BLPI measure in the long-line regime, while short lines exhibit multimode or single-mode behavior.
AI.motivation: To understand the reduced dynamics of superconducting qubits coupled through finite-length transmission lines and to establish a unified circuit QED picture bridging continuum (structured reservoir) and discrete (few-mode coupler) regimes.
AI.method: Circuit quantization starting from a lumped-element model; decomposition of line modes into even- and odd-parity sectors coupled to collective qubit operators; Drude-Lorentz spectral density for each sector in the continuum limit; hierarchical equations of motion (HEOM) for dynamics; Breuer-Laine-Piilo (BLPI) measure for non-Markovianity.
AI.result: Identified parameter regions (based on ω_q, ω_TL, ω_g hierarchy) where the transmission line acts as a structured reservoir with non-Markovian relaxation (long-line continuum limit) versus discrete few-mode coupler (short-line limit). Non-Markovianity is quantified via the BLPI measure.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a unified cQED framework capturing the crossover from a structured reservoir (long line) to a discrete multimode or single-mode coupler (short line), offering guidance for engineering qubit-qubit interactions and decoherence in superconducting quantum circuits.

title: LightStim: A Framework for QEC Protocol Evaluation and Prototyping with Automated DEM Construction
summary: Fault-tolerant quantum computing increasingly demands rigorous, circuit-level evaluation of diverse quantum error correction (QEC) protocols and efficient prototyping of new ones. Such evaluation requires both the physical circuit and its Detector Error Model (DEM) to simulate end-to-end logical error rates. However, DEM construction today is performed by manual annotation, a tedious and error-prone process that effectively limits evaluation to simple memory experiments. We present LightStim, a framework that automates DEM construction concurrently with circuit compilation by maintaining a Pauli tableau augmented with measurement records, with no protocol-specific input required. We benchmark LightStim across protocols from memory experiments to end-to-end distillation circuits; cross-validation against public implementations confirms exact detector and observable counts and consistent logical error rates. LightStim additionally accelerates the exploration of new protocols, which we demonstrate through a novel heterogeneous cross-code lattice surgery design between surface and punctured quantum Reed-Muller codes. These capabilities together make LightStim a unified infrastructure for systematic QEC protocol evaluation and exploration.

AI.tldr: LightStim automates Detector Error Model (DEM) construction during circuit compilation using a Pauli tableau with measurement records, enabling scalable and error-free evaluation of diverse QEC protocols without manual annotation.
AI.motivation: Manual DEM construction is tedious, error-prone, and limits QEC evaluation to simple memory experiments, creating a bottleneck for rigorous circuit-level evaluation of diverse quantum error correction protocols.
AI.method: LightStim automates DEM construction concurrently with circuit compilation by maintaining a Pauli tableau augmented with measurement records, requiring no protocol-specific input.
AI.result: Cross-validation against public implementations confirms exact detector and observable counts and consistent logical error rates across protocols from memory experiments to end-to-end distillation circuits.
AI.conclusion: LightStim provides a unified infrastructure for systematic QEC protocol evaluation and exploration, demonstrated through a novel heterogeneous cross-code lattice surgery design between surface and punctured quantum Reed-Muller codes.

title: Suppressing the Erasure Error of Fusion Operation in Photonic Quantum Computing
summary:
Photonic quantum computing provides a promising route toward quantum computation by naturally supporting the measurement-based quantum computation (MBQC) model. In MBQC, programs are executed through measurements on a pre-generated graph state, whose construction largely depends on probabilistic fusion operations. However, fusion operations in PQC are vulnerable to two major error sources: fusion failure and fusion erasure. As a result, MBQC compilation must account for both error mechanisms to generate reliable and efficient photonic executions. Prior state-of-the-art MBQC compilation, represented by OneAdapt, is designed for all-photonic architectures and mainly focuses on handling fusion failures. Nevertheless, it does not explicitly model fusion erasures induced by photon loss, which can be substantially more damaging than fusion failures.
  To mitigate fusion erasure errors, we introduce a new MBQC compilation scheme built upon the spin qubit quantum memory. We propose tree-encoded fusion, an encoding strategy that suppresses erasure errors during graph-state generation. We further incorporate this scheme into a compiler framework with algorithms that reduce the execution overhead of quantum programs. We evaluate the proposed framework using a realistic PQC simulator on six representative quantum algorithm benchmarks across multiple program scales. The results show that tree-encoded fusion achieves better robustness than alternative fusion-encoding strategies, and that our compiler provides exponential improvement over OneAdapt. In addition, we validate the feasibility of our approach through a proof-of-concept demonstration on real PQC hardware.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces tree-encoded fusion, a spin-qubit-memory-based MBQC compilation scheme that explicitly models and mitigates fusion erasure errors in photonic quantum computing, achieving exponential improvement over prior state-of-the-art OneAdapt.
AI.motivation: Prior MBQC compilation (OneAdapt) handles fusion failures but ignores fusion erasures caused by photon loss, which are substantially more damaging in photonic quantum computing.
AI.method: The authors propose tree-encoded fusion, an encoding strategy that suppresses erasure errors during graph-state generation, integrated into a compiler framework with algorithms to reduce execution overhead, built upon spin qubit quantum memory.
AI.result: Tree-encoded fusion achieves better robustness than alternative fusion-encoding strategies; the compiler provides exponential improvement over OneAdapt across six benchmarks; feasibility validated on real PQC hardware.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling fusion erasure errors and using tree-encoded fusion with spin qubit memory enables significantly more robust and efficient MBQC compilation for photonic quantum computing.

title: Catalytic quantum thermodynamics beyond additivity and reduced-state monotones
summary: The generalized second laws of quantum thermodynamics are usually formulated in terms of Rényi divergences and the associated family of generalized free energies. In catalytic thermal transformations, this framework typically certifies the existence of a suitable catalyst but does not make the catalytic contribution explicit in the resulting system-level inequalities. Here we develop a complementary formulation based on non-additive divergences, whose pseudo-additive structure yields a family of generalized free energies with an explicit catalyst-dependent correction term. For uncorrelated catalytic thermal transformations, we show that this leads to non-additive second-law relations that make the catalytic contribution explicit and provide nontrivial constraints on admissible catalysts when the catalyst is returned only approximately. We also analyze correlated catalytic thermal transformations and show, through explicit finite-dimensional examples, that reduced-state data are generally insufficient to characterize thermodynamic accessibility: the thermo-majorization behavior of the joint transformation can change while the system and catalyst marginals remain fixed, and even states with identical marginals and the same mutual information can exhibit different thermo-majorization accessibility. Our results show that non-additivity can be thermodynamically informative in uncorrelated catalysis, whereas correlated catalysis generally requires a genuinely joint-state-sensitive description beyond reduced-state monotones.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a complementary formulation of generalized second laws using non-additive divergences, which makes catalytic contributions explicit in uncorrelated transformations. It shows that correlated catalysis requires joint-state-sensitive descriptions, as reduced-state data (including marginals and mutual information) are insufficient to determine thermodynamic accessibility.
AI.motivation: Standard generalized second laws (formulated via Rényi divergences) certify the existence of a catalyst but do not make the catalytic contribution explicit in system-level inequalities, leaving the role of the catalyst implicit.
AI.method: The authors employ non-additive divergences whose pseudo-additive structure yields generalized free energies with an explicit catalyst-dependent correction term. They analyze both uncorrelated and correlated catalytic thermal transformations using finite-dimensional examples.
AI.result: For uncorrelated catalysis, non-additive second-law relations make the catalytic contribution explicit and constrain admissible catalysts (especially under approximate return). For correlated catalysis, reduced-state data (marginals, mutual information) are insufficient: thermo-majorization can differ even when marginals and mutual information are identical.
AI.conclusion: Non-additivity is thermodynamically informative in uncorrelated catalysis, while correlated catalysis requires a genuinely joint-state-sensitive approach beyond reduced-state monotones.

title: Quantum plasmonics with N emitters: bright hybrid continuum selection
summary: We construct mode-selective effective models describing the interaction of the quantum plasmon-polariton field supported by a finite dielectric medium and one or several quantum emitters. The construction of the effective model is based on the decomposition of the field into bright modes relevant to the interaction with the emitters and dark modes, which do not interact with the emitters. We show that the quantum plasmon-polariton field can be represented equivalently by a double-continuum spectrum or by a single hybrid continuum spectrum for each emitter. The system of the electromagnetic field coupled to a finite medium is composed of two families of continuum modes, each of them with an infinite degeneracy. The two families are deformations of the free electromagnetic field and the free medium, induced by the interaction between them, as described by the Lippmann-Schwinger equations. We show that if there are $N$ emitters interacting with this plasmon-polariton field, the effective interaction involves a much smaller set of bosonic continuum modes: the interacting part of the continuum can be described by $N$ non-degenerate one-dimensional continua, one for each emitter. The representation of the interaction in terms of a single hybrid continuum spectrum coincides with the one within the macroscopic Langevin model with bulk medium. This coincidence is explained by an exact compensation of two terms, one in the coupling term of the Hamiltonian and the other one in a Green tensor identity.

AI.tldr: The paper develops mode-selective effective models for quantum plasmon-polariton fields interacting with quantum emitters, showing that the complex double-continuum spectrum can be reduced to a single hybrid continuum per emitter, and that this representation matches the macroscopic Langevin model due to exact compensation between coupling and Green tensor terms.
AI.motivation: To construct simplified, mode-selective effective models that capture the essential physics of quantum plasmon-polariton fields interacting with quantum emitters, avoiding the complexity of the full double-continuum spectrum.
AI.method: Decomposition of the plasmon-polariton field into bright modes (interacting with emitters) and dark modes (non-interacting). The field is represented equivalently by a double-continuum spectrum or a single hybrid continuum per emitter. Lippmann-Schwinger equations describe the deformation of free fields due to their interaction. For N emitters, the effective interaction reduces to N non-degenerate one-dimensional continua.
AI.result: The single hybrid continuum representation coincides with the macroscopic Langevin model with bulk medium. This coincidence is explained by an exact compensation between a term in the Hamiltonian coupling and a term in a Green tensor identity.
AI.conclusion: The effective mode-selective model successfully reduces the complexity of quantum plasmon-polariton interactions, bridging microscopic and macroscopic (Langevin) descriptions of the system.

title: Pulse Shaping for Superconducting Qubits
summary: High-fidelity control of superconducting qubits requires carefully shaped microwave pulses that account for multiple error channels. In this work, we present a pedagogical introduction to pulse-shaping techniques for transmon qubits, aiming to provide a unified, accessible framework that integrates physical intuition for pulse design, analytical understanding of gate-level descriptions, and practical considerations of hardware. This article further aims to serve as a guide for students and early researchers entering superconducting quantum computing. We begin by examining simple pulse envelopes and their spectral properties, highlighting how finite bandwidth leads to leakage outside the computational subspace. These observations motivate the introduction of the derivative removal by adiabatic gate (DRAG) technique, which uses a quadrature component proportional to the pulse's time derivative to suppress off-resonant excitations. We analyze the single-qubit case using the Magnus expansion, which provides a clear understanding of the order-by-order introduction of error channels. We discuss the practical hardware realities of control pulse generation, focusing on arbitrary waveform generators (AWG), local oscillators (LO), and IQ mixing. Common imperfections are discussed in terms of their impact on the effective pulse shape and qubit Hamiltonian. Finally, we extend the discussion to two-qubit operations, focusing on the cross-resonance gate and the emergence of effective interactions.

AI.tldr: This paper provides a pedagogical introduction to pulse-shaping techniques for superconducting transmon qubits, covering DRAG, Magnus expansion analysis, hardware realities (AWG, LO, IQ mixing), and two-qubit cross-resonance gates, aimed at students and early researchers.
AI.motivation: To provide a unified, accessible framework for pulse-shaping that integrates physical intuition, analytical gate-level descriptions, and practical hardware considerations, serving as a guide for newcomers to superconducting quantum computing.
AI.method: Pedagogical exposition: starts with simple pulse envelopes and spectral properties; introduces DRAG (derivative removal by adiabatic gate) with quadrature component; applies Magnus expansion for order-by-order error analysis; discusses AWG, LO, and IQ mixing hardware realities; extends to two-qubit cross-resonance gates.
AI.result: Demonstrates how finite bandwidth causes leakage errors and how DRAG suppresses off-resonant excitations; provides clear error channel understanding via Magnus expansion; characterizes hardware imperfections' impact on pulse shape and Hamiltonian; shows emergence of effective interactions in cross-resonance gates.
AI.conclusion: The article serves as a comprehensive pedagogical guide that bridges physical intuition, analytical methods, and practical hardware considerations for high-fidelity control of superconducting qubits via pulse shaping.

title: Composite quantum gates simultaneously compensated for multiple errors
summary: Systematic control errors remain a primary obstacle to realizing high-fidelity single-qubit gates. We introduce composite pulse sequences that implement X and Hadamard gates while simultaneously compensating amplitude (Rabi-frequency), detuning (frequency), and duration errors. Our construction uses two complementary strategies: (i) derivative-based cancellation of error terms in the full unitary (not just the transition probability), formulated via the Cayley-Klein parametrization, and (ii) direct minimization of the average gate infidelity over prescribed error ranges. We derive symmetric five-pulse solutions with closed-form phases that cancel all first-order terms (including the mixed derivative), and numerically optimize longer sequences -- up to 15 pulses -- to achieve higher-order suppression. We also show that standard ``universal'' five-pulse sequences (U5a/U5b) emerge as simple phase-shifted instances of our symmetric solutions, yielding broad robustness to both detuning and amplitude errors. Finally, we construct variable-area sequences for $R_x(π/2)$, which, up to virtual Z rotations, benchmark the Hadamard gate. Across all families we observe the expected trade-off between sequence length and robustness window, with substantial boosts in fidelity over large error domains.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces composite pulse sequences (symmetric 5-pulse closed-form and numerically optimized up to 15 pulses) that simultaneously compensate amplitude, detuning, and duration errors for high-fidelity X and Hadamard gates, using derivative-based cancellation via Cayley-Klein parametrization and direct infidelity minimization.
AI.motivation: Systematic control errors (amplitude, detuning, duration) are the primary obstacle to realizing high-fidelity single-qubit gates.
AI.method: Two complementary strategies: (i) derivative-based cancellation of error terms in the full unitary via Cayley-Klein parametrization, and (ii) direct minimization of average gate infidelity over prescribed error ranges.
AI.result: Symmetric five-pulse solutions with closed-form phases cancel all first-order terms including mixed derivatives; numerically optimized sequences up to 15 pulses achieve higher-order suppression; standard U5a/U5b sequences emerge as phase-shifted instances of symmetric solutions.
AI.conclusion: Trade-off between sequence length and robustness window is observed, with substantial fidelity boosts over large error domains.

title: Speed-oriented quantum circuit backend
summary: We present a new software package for efficient quantum circuit generation, designed to achieve optimal runtime performance. Despite being in an early stage of development, our implementation demonstrates significant advantages over existing tools. Using the quantum Fourier transform (QFT) as a benchmark, we show that our backend can generate circuits for systems with up to 2000 qubits faster than widely used frameworks such as Qiskit and Q#. This improvement is particularly relevant for applications where classical preprocessing time, including circuit generation, must be minimized to not diminish any potential quantum advantage - for example, in combinatorial optimization tasks. Additionally, our software provides high-level primitives for bit- and integer-level manipulations, offering a simplified interface for integration with high-level quantum programming languages.

AI.tldr: A new quantum circuit generation package achieves optimal runtime performance, generating circuits for up to 2000 qubits faster than Qiskit and Q#, while offering high-level primitives for simplified integration with quantum programming languages.
AI.motivation: Classical preprocessing time (including circuit generation) must be minimized to not diminish potential quantum advantage, especially in applications like combinatorial optimization.
AI.method: A software package designed for optimal runtime performance with high-level primitives for bit- and integer-level manipulations, benchmarked using quantum Fourier transform (QFT).
AI.result: The backend generates circuits for up to 2000 qubits faster than Qiskit and Q#.
AI.conclusion: The proposed package outperforms widely used frameworks in circuit generation speed and provides a simplified interface for integration with high-level quantum programming languages.

title: Spectral Diffusion Mitigation with a Laser Pulse Sequence
summary: The optical spectrum of a quantum system is jointly determined by the properties of the emitter and the driving field. All-optical spectral control can hence be a promising method to engineer the properties of single photon emitters for quantum technological applications. It was proposed that driving a two-level system with a periodic sequence of optical pi-pulses during the excited state lifetime shifts the emission and absorption maximum to an arbitrarily detuned pulse carrier frequency, enabling the mitigation of spectral diffusion in noisy emitters. In this article, we report on the first experimental observation of this effect. We implement the protocol on a solid-state emitter and reduce its inhomogeneously broadened optical linewidth close to the lifetime limit. By detuning the excitation laser, we are able to concentrate approximately half of the absorption to a freely selectable target frequency. Our approach is solely based on properties of coherently evolving quantum systems, rendering it applicable to a wide range of individual and ensembles of quantum emitters.

AI.tldr: The paper reports the first experimental observation of using periodic optical pi-pulse sequences to shift emission/absorption spectra and reduce inhomogeneous broadening in solid-state quantum emitters, achieving near-lifetime-limited linewidths and tunable absorption frequency.
AI.motivation: To experimentally demonstrate a proposed all-optical method for spectral control of quantum emitters, specifically to mitigate spectral diffusion in noisy emitters by driving a two-level system with periodic pi-pulses during its excited state lifetime.
AI.method: The protocol was implemented on a solid-state emitter using a periodic sequence of optical pi-pulses. The excitation laser frequency was detuned to control the absorption maximum, and the inhomogeneously broadened optical linewidth was measured.
AI.result: The inhomogeneously broadened optical linewidth was reduced close to the lifetime limit. By detuning the excitation laser, approximately half of the absorption could be concentrated at a freely selectable target frequency.
AI.conclusion: The approach is solely based on properties of coherently evolving quantum systems, making it applicable to a wide range of individual and ensembles of quantum emitters.

title: Bipartite entanglement under frequency comb pumping in parametric Josephson circuits
summary: The creation of high-quality cluster states in superconducting microwave circuits is a relevant ingredient in continuous-variable quantum computing. Although large-scale cluster states have been established in optical systems, dissipation prevents their direct applicability to the microwave realm. Recent improvements in superconducting parametric circuits, in particular Josephson parametric amplifiers (JPA) and traveling wave parametric amplifiers (TWPA), have permitted substantial progress in producing entangled states using microwave photons. In this paper, we examine experimentally and theoretically the effects of numerous parametric pump tones on the degree of two-mode squeezing in a quantum circuit and apply it to the JPA. We find that additional pumps diminish the initial two-mode correlations achieved with a single pump by redistributing it among a larger network of modes and by introducing entanglement with additional idler frequencies. Taking into account the actual heterodyne measurement conditions, the experimental results are consistent with theoretical expectations.

AI.tldr: This paper experimentally and theoretically investigates how multiple parametric pump tones affect two-mode squeezing in a Josephson parametric amplifier, showing that additional pumps reduce initial correlations by redistributing entanglement across more modes.
AI.motivation: To advance continuous-variable quantum computing in the microwave regime by understanding how to create high-quality cluster states using superconducting circuits, specifically addressing the challenge that dissipation hinders direct translation of optical cluster state techniques to microwave systems.
AI.method: Experimental and theoretical examination of multiple parametric pump tones' effects on two-mode squeezing in a quantum circuit, applied to a Josephson parametric amplifier (JPA), with results compared to heterodyne measurement conditions.
AI.result: Additional pump tones degrade the two-mode squeezing achieved with a single pump by redistributing correlations among a larger network of modes and introducing entanglement with extra idler frequencies; experimental results match theoretical predictions under actual measurement conditions.
AI.conclusion: Multiple pumps in a JPA reduce initial two-mode correlations, which must be accounted for when designing microwave-based entangled states for continuous-variable quantum computing.

title: Lagrange: Operating Italy's First Publicly-Accessible Quantum Computer for Research and Education
summary: We describe the design, implementation, and nine-month operational experience of the software management stack for Lagrange, an IQM Spark five-qubit superconducting quantum computer jointly acquired by LINKS Foundation, Politecnico di Torino and the Italian National Institute of Metrological Research (INRiM), and managed by LINKS. Lagrange is, to our knowledge, the first quantum computer in Italy that is fully operational and accessible to students and researchers from multiple institutions under formal service agreements, and to the general public under commercial agreements. When installed in mid-2025, the IQM Spark hardware was delivered by the vendor with authentication only: no billing, project management or fair usage enforcement were provided. We developed a modular middleware layer that filled that gap without modifying any vendor client software, by intercepting API calls through a proxy that enforces project-based budgets, reservation-aware authorisation, and per-user fairness policies. The middleware adopts a plugin architecture that cleanly separates vendor-specific logic from site-specific policies, enabling reuse across different quantum hardware backends and deployment contexts. Since entering production in September 2025, the system has processed over 240,000 quantum jobs totalling more than 1 week of QPU execution time, with greater than 98% uptime. Notably, students at Politecnico di Torino regularly use the machine during both lectures and formal examinations -- a practice we believe to be unique in Europe. We report on the system architecture, the operational lessons learned, and the infrastructure choices that made this deployment possible.

AI.tldr: This paper describes the design, implementation, and nine-month operation of the software management stack for Lagrange, Italy's first fully operational superconducting quantum computer, which serves students, researchers, and the public under service agreements.
AI.motivation: To fill the gap in billing, project management, and fair usage enforcement on a vendor-delivered IQM Spark quantum computer that only provided authentication, enabling multi-institutional and public access.
AI.method: A modular middleware layer deployed as a proxy that intercepts API calls to enforce project-based budgets, reservation-aware authorization, and per-user fairness policies, using a plugin architecture separating vendor-specific logic from site-specific policies without modifying vendor software.
AI.result: Over 240,000 quantum jobs processed (more than 1 week of QPU execution time), greater than 98% uptime, and regular use by students during lectures and formal examinations at Politecnico di Torino.
AI.conclusion: The middleware successfully enabled multi-tenant, policy-enforced access to a quantum computer with high reliability, and the modular design supports reuse across different hardware backends and deployment contexts.

title: Testing Spontaneous Collapse Models with Coulomb Mediated Squeezing
summary: We show that detecting steady-state Coulomb-mediated reduction in the thermal variance of the differential motional mode of two nanospheres can bound the Continuous Spontaneous Localization (CSL) parameter ($λ_{\text{CSL}}$). For realistic experimental parameters, the resulting bounds are comparable to those obtained from X-ray emission experiments and surpass those set by bulk-heating ones. Unlike these latter experiments, our bounds are robust against plausible coloured-noise extensions of collapse models. In the short-time regime, we find that a weak Coulomb-induced entanglement-based test between two charged nanospheres initialized in ground state can provide constraints on $λ_{\text{CSL}}$ comparable to limits set by early X-ray experiments.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes using Coulomb-mediated thermal variance reduction in two nanospheres to bound the CSL parameter (λ_CSL), achieving constraints comparable to X-ray experiments and surpassing bulk-heating ones, with robustness against colored-noise extensions and further improvements from entanglement-based tests.
AI.motivation: To develop a novel experimental approach for bounding the Continuous Spontaneous Localization (CSL) parameter that overcomes limitations of existing methods, particularly sensitivity to colored-noise extensions of collapse models.
AI.method: Analyzing two regimes: (1) steady-state detection of Coulomb-mediated reduction in thermal variance of the differential motional mode of two nanospheres, and (2) short-time Coulomb-induced entanglement-based test between two charged nanospheres initialized in ground state.
AI.result: Steady-state bounds on λ_CSL are comparable to X-ray emission experiments and surpass bulk-heating bounds, while being robust against colored-noise extensions. Short-time entanglement bounds are comparable to early X-ray experiments.
AI.conclusion: Coulomb-mediated nanosphere experiments offer competitive and robust constraints on CSL collapse models, addressing key weaknesses of existing experimental approaches.

title: Near-Term Reduction in Nonlocal Gate Count from Distributed Logical Qubits
summary: Modular quantum computing architectures require error correction schemes that remain effective in the presence of noisy inter-processor operations. As such, minimizing the number of such operations on logical circuits partitioned across quantum processors is a primary objective of distributed quantum computing. In this work, we develop basic techniques for qubit allocation using an exemplar color code family and explore generalizations to other color codes. In particular, we show that a 10% reduction in processor-nonlocal gates is achievable in a setting where syndrome extraction occurs after every logical gate, as in today's devices, and that this scales to significantly greater advantages in the multi-qubit case. We also explore methods of achieving universal gate sets efficiently in this distributed logical setting and evaluate the trade-offs of multiple approaches such as magic state distillation, code switching, and a new method based on logical swaps. Finally, we discuss some considerations for an allocation algorithm for these architectures to perform scalably and connect it to existing work on quantum circuit partitions.

AI.tldr: This paper develops qubit allocation techniques for distributed quantum computing using color codes, demonstrating a 10% reduction in non-local gates per logical gate, scaling to greater advantages in multi-qubit cases. It compares methods for universal gate sets (magic state distillation, code switching, logical swaps) and discusses allocation algorithms for modular architectures.
AI.motivation: Modular quantum computing architectures require error correction schemes that remain effective despite noisy inter-processor operations, motivating the need to minimize such operations on logical circuits partitioned across quantum processors.
AI.method: Develops basic qubit allocation techniques using an exemplar color code family, explores generalizations to other color codes, and evaluates three approaches for universal gate sets: magic state distillation, code switching, and a new method based on logical swaps.
AI.result: Demonstrates a 10% reduction in processor-nonlocal gates in a setting with syndrome extraction after every logical gate, which scales to significantly greater advantages in multi-qubit cases.
AI.conclusion: The proposed allocation techniques and universal gate set methods offer practical trade-offs for distributed quantum computing, with connections to existing work on quantum circuit partitions for scalable architectures.

title: Entanglement of two optical emitters mediated by a terahertz channel
summary: Quantum technologies in the terahertz (THz) require a coherent interface between addressable qubits and THz quantum channels -- a capacity that so far, remains largely underdeveloped. Here, we propose and demonstrate the generation of steady-state entanglement between polar quantum emitters, mediated by THz photons. We exploit strong visible-light driving of the emitters to create Rabi-split dressed eigenstates whose energy separation can be optically tuned into the THz regime. The polar nature of the emitters activates THz transitions within these eigenstates, allowing them to couple to a THz photonic mode that induces collective dissipative dynamics. A coherent driving and control of these effective THz emitters is achieved by using a sideband optical drive with detuning close to the THz transition frequency. The resulting interplay of collective dissipation and driving activates a mechanism to generate steady-state entanglement with high values of the concurrence ($C>0.9$), attainable under experimentally feasible parameters. Crucially, both coherent manipulation and quantum state tomography are implemented entirely through optical means, avoiding direct THz control and detection. This establishes a hybrid visible-THz quantum interface in which a THz channel mediates qubit-qubit entanglement (a key operational requirement for THz quantum technologies) while remaining optically accessible.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes and demonstrates steady-state entanglement between polar quantum emitters mediated by THz photons, using all-optical control to achieve concurrence >0.9, establishing a hybrid visible-THz quantum interface for THz quantum technologies.
AI.motivation: There is a lack of coherent interfaces between addressable qubits and THz quantum channels, which is essential for advancing quantum technologies in the terahertz regime.
AI.method: Strong visible-light driving creates Rabi-split dressed eigenstates with tunable energy separation in the THz regime; polar emitters enable THz transitions coupling to a THz photonic mode, inducing collective dissipative dynamics; a sideband optical drive with detuning near the THz transition provides coherent control.
AI.result: Steady-state entanglement with concurrence >0.9 is achieved under experimentally feasible parameters; coherent manipulation and quantum state tomography are implemented entirely through optical means without direct THz control or detection.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes a hybrid visible-THz quantum interface where a THz channel mediates qubit-qubit entanglement while remaining optically accessible, a key operational requirement for THz quantum technologies.

title: Symplectic split-operator method for the time-dependent unitary Tavis-Cummings model
summary: We present a fast, memory-efficient, unitarity-preserving numerical method beyond the rotating-wave approximation for the closed Tavis-Cummings model in which a multilevel spin system interacts with a cavity mode. This model can describe the interaction of an ensemble of spins with a cavity mode in which the spin frequency and other parameters are time-dependent. The method exploits the fact that, while the Tavis-Cummings model is not tri-diagonal, it can be brought into tri-diagonal form by a change of basis that can be implemented purely by re-indexing (permuting basis elements), which is a fast operation. By truncating the Fock basis of the cavity mode, the computational complexity of the method is linear in the total dimension of the coupled system, both in time and memory. The method can be employed to simulate any closed quantum system whose Hamiltonian terms can be brought into tri-diagonal form.

AI.tldr: A fast, memory-efficient method for simulating the closed Tavis-Cummings model beyond the rotating-wave approximation by transforming the Hamiltonian into tri-diagonal form via pure basis permutation, achieving linear computational complexity in the system dimension.
AI.motivation: To develop a numerical method for the Tavis-Cummings model that is both efficient (linear in time and memory) and preserves unitarity beyond the rotating-wave approximation, addressing the challenge that the standard Tavis-Cummings Hamiltonian is not tri-diagonal.
AI.method: The Hamiltonian is transformed into tri-diagonal form via a change of basis implemented purely by re-indexing (permuting basis elements), then the Fock basis of the cavity mode is truncated, resulting in computational complexity linear in the total dimension of the coupled system.
AI.result: The method achieves linear computational complexity in both time and memory with respect to the total dimension of the coupled system, and preserves unitarity.
AI.conclusion: The method can simulate any closed quantum system whose Hamiltonian terms can be brought into tri-diagonal form, and is applicable to the Tavis-Cummings model with time-dependent spin frequency and other parameters beyond the rotating-wave approximation.

title: Partial oracles quantum algorithm framework -- Part I: Analysis of in-place operations
summary: The partial oracles framework is a quantum search algorithm that has the potential to exceed the quadratic speedup of Grover's algorithm, up to a theoretical maximum of an exponential speedup. Until now, however, the framework has lacked an explicit method for constructing the operator that represents the search iteration. In this paper, we provide the missing construction, for the special case of an oracle function definable using only in-place operations (that is, where the calculated result of the oracle function can be read just from the qubits in the search index). The restriction to in-place operations means that the current work does not yet exhibit quantum advantage: oracle functions constructed using only in-place operations are always classically reversible. To demonstrate quantum advantage, it will be necessary to extend this construction method to include out-of-place operations (part II). As part of the construction of the search iteration operator, we define a new type of transform, the reciprocal transform, which is applied to the oracle function. We show that the reciprocal transform obeys a chain rule, which makes it possible to break down complex transforms into simple steps. To illustrate the practical application of this search method, we apply the reciprocal transform to elementary operations from the SHA-256 hash algorithm: addition modulo $2^n$, the $Maj(a, b, c)$ function, the $Ch(a, b, c)$ function, and the bit shift functions. We also introduce the QFrame python library, which is used to automate the construction of quantum circuits that represent reciprocal transforms.

AI.tldr: This paper provides the missing construction for the search iteration operator in the partial oracles framework for quantum search, specifically for in-place oracle functions. It introduces the reciprocal transform and demonstrates its application to SHA-256 elementary operations, along with the QFrame python library for automating circuit construction.
AI.motivation: The partial oracles framework promises potential exponential speedup over Grover's algorithm, but lacked an explicit method for constructing the search iteration operator. This work aims to fill that gap.
AI.method: The authors provide a construction for the search iteration operator for in-place oracle functions, define a new reciprocal transform with a chain rule property, and implement the QFrame Python library for automated circuit construction.
AI.result: The reciprocal transform is shown to obey a chain rule enabling decomposition. Applications to SHA-256 operations (addition modulo 2^n, Maj, Ch, bit shifts) are demonstrated. The QFrame library automates reciprocal transform circuit construction.
AI.conclusion: This work provides the construction for in-place oracle functions but does not yet exhibit quantum advantage since in-place operations are classically reversible. Extension to out-of-place operations (part II) is needed for quantum advantage.

title: Rigorous Security Proofs for Practical Quantum Key Distribution
comment: PhD thesis. Based on arXiv:2601.18035, arXiv:2601.17960, arXiv:2408.17349, arXiv:2403.11851, arXiv:2311.01600
summary: This thesis is concerned with rigorous security analyses of practical Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) protocols, using a variety of modern proof techniques. The main results are as follows. First, we establish a security proof for variable-length QKD protocols against IID collective attacks, and extend this result to coherent attacks using the postselection technique. In doing so, we resolve a long-standing flaw in the application of the postselection technique to QKD, thereby placing it on a rigorous mathematical footing. Second, we develop a method to bound phase error rates in entropic uncertainty relation-based and phase error rate-based proofs, using only the observed statistics of the protocol, even when detectors are imperfect and only approximately characterized. This removes a key assumption of identical detector behaviour and enables these techniques to be applied in realistic settings. Third, we present a very general security analysis based on the marginal-constrained entropy accumulation theorem. The resulting framework can be readily adapted to practical imperfections and side channels, and is suitable for certification efforts. Finally, we show that the security of QKD protocols under realistic authentication assumptions can be reduced to the standard idealized setting, where authentication is assumed to behave honestly, with only minor protocol modifications. A distinctive feature of this thesis is its unified presentation of several major QKD security proof frameworks using consistent protocol descriptions and notation. Consequently, this thesis is intended not only as a collection of new technical results, but also as a useful reference for understanding rigorous security analysis in quantum key distribution.

AI.tldr: This thesis provides rigorous security proofs for practical QKD protocols, resolving a long-standing flaw in the postselection technique, removing the identical-detector assumption in phase-error analysis, developing a general framework using marginal-constrained entropy accumulation, and reducing realistic authentication to idealized settings—all presented in a unified notation.
AI.motivation: To place QKD security proofs on rigorous mathematical footing, resolve known flaws (e.g., postselection technique), remove impractical assumptions (e.g., identical detector behavior), and develop frameworks adaptable to realistic imperfections and side channels.
AI.method: Uses postselection technique to extend IID collective attack proofs to coherent attacks; develops bounding methods for phase error rates using only observed statistics via entropic uncertainty relations; applies marginal-constrained entropy accumulation theorem for general security analysis; reduces realistic authentication to idealized settings via protocol modifications.
AI.result: Resolved a long-standing flaw in postselection technique application to QKD; removed the identical-detector assumption in phase error rate proofs; established a general and adaptable security analysis framework; showed realistic authentication security can be reduced to idealized settings with minor modifications.
AI.conclusion: The thesis provides a rigorous, unified reference for QKD security analysis, with results that are practically adaptable to real-world imperfections and side channels.

title: Variance Geometry of Exact Pauli-Detecting Codes: Continuous Landscapes Beyond Stabilizers
summary: Exact quantum codes detecting a prescribed set of Pauli errors are approached through algebraic constructions--stabilizer, codeword-stabilized, permutation-invariant, topological, and related families. Geometrically, exact Pauli detection is governed by joint higher-rank numerical ranges of these Pauli operators, whose structure for rank $\geq 2$ is largely uncharted. From this viewpoint, we show that such codes often form connected continuous families rather than collections of disjoint solution regions. These families are characterized by a single scalar derived from the Knill-Laflamme conditions: denoted $λ^*$, it is the Euclidean norm of the signature vector of Pauli expectation values on the maximally mixed code state, and provides a one-parameter summary of the code's joint Pauli variance profile. Within these continuous landscapes, stabilizer codes occupy only discrete, measure-zero subsets of the attainable $λ^*$-spectrum, exposing a largely unexplored continuum of genuinely nonadditive exact codes. We establish this picture by analyzing the geometry of higher-rank operator compressions, and extend it to symmetry-restricted settings where cyclic and permutation symmetries are imposed on both the error model and the code projector. Small-system cases reveal interval, singleton, and empty regimes through eigenvalue interlacing and symmetry-sector decompositions; larger systems are treated numerically via Stiefel-manifold optimization and symmetry-adapted parameterizations. In every unrestricted and symmetry-compatible case analyzed, the attainable $λ^*$-spectrum forms a single closed interval whenever nonempty--although a general proof remains open. These results place stabilizer, symmetric, and nonadditive code families within a unified higher-rank variance framework, suggesting a continuous geometric perspective on the landscape of exact quantum codes.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that exact quantum codes detecting prescribed Pauli errors form continuous connected families rather than isolated solutions, characterized by a single scalar λ* (the Euclidean norm of the Pauli expectation signature vector on the maximally mixed state). Stabilizer codes are discrete, measure-zero subsets within this continuum, revealing a vast landscape of nonadditive exact codes. The geometry is analyzed via higher-rank operator compressions, symmetry-restricted settings, eigenvalue interlacing, and Stiefel-manifold optimization.
AI.motivation: To unify stabilizer, codeword-stabilized, permutation-invariant, topological, and nonadditive quantum codes within a single geometric framework, and to reveal the largely unexplored continuous landscape of exact quantum codes beyond the discrete stabilizer paradigm.
AI.method: The paper employs joint higher-rank numerical ranges of Pauli operators, the Knill-Laflamme conditions reduced to a scalar λ* (norm of the Pauli expectation signature vector), eigenvalue interlacing and symmetry-sector decompositions for small systems, and Stiefel-manifold optimization with symmetry-adapted parameterizations for larger systems.
AI.result: Exact quantum codes form connected continuous families (single closed intervals in λ*-spectrum) rather than isolated solution regions. Stabilizer codes occupy only discrete, measure-zero subsets of this spectrum. Cyclic and permutation symmetries produce interval, singleton, or empty regimes via interlacing.
AI.conclusion: The work establishes a unified higher-rank variance framework for stabilizer, symmetric, and nonadditive code families, suggesting a continuous geometric perspective on exact quantum error correction where λ* serves as a one-parameter summary of the joint Pauli variance profile.

title: Robust continuous symmetry breaking and multiversality in the chiral Dicke model
comment: 4 main pages + 3 suppl., 4 main figures + 1 suppl; comments are welcome
summary: The Dicke model (DM) serves as a paradigm for understanding collective light-matter interactions. We introduce the chiral Dicke model, a generalization where an atomic ensemble couples to a two-mode cavity via chiral interactions. Unlike the standard DM, the chiral DM is endowed with an inherent continuous $U(1)$ symmetry associated with angular momentum conservation. The ground-state phase diagram and the associated quantum phase transitions are charted out, revealing a $U(1)$-broken superradiant phase that spans a broad parameter space. We demonstrate that the spectrum of quantum fluctuations is highly tunable in both the symmetric and broken phases. Strikingly, our calculations reveal that the system exhibits `multiversality', where distinct universality classes govern the transition between the same two phases. In particular, along a special line in parameter space, the dynamical critical exponent for the normal-superradiant phase transition changes from $zν=1$ to $zν=1/2$. Our work establishes the chiral Dicke model as a powerful platform to realize novel quantum phases and multiversal critical phenomena in light-matter coupled systems.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the chiral Dicke model, a generalization of the Dicke model where an atomic ensemble couples to a two-mode cavity via chiral interactions, revealing a U(1)-broken superradiant phase, highly tunable quantum fluctuations, and multiversality with distinct universality classes (dynamical critical exponent changing from zν=1 to zν=1/2) governing the same phase transition.
AI.motivation: To generalize the standard Dicke model by incorporating chiral light-matter interactions, enabling the study of novel quantum phases and critical phenomena beyond conventional collective light-matter coupling paradigms.
AI.method: The chiral Dicke model is formulated with a two-mode cavity and chiral coupling, leveraging an inherent U(1) symmetry from angular momentum conservation. The ground-state phase diagram and quantum phase transitions are analyzed theoretically, with calculations of quantum fluctuation spectra and critical exponents.
AI.result: A U(1)-broken superradiant phase exists over a broad parameter range; the quantum fluctuation spectrum is highly tunable; the system exhibits multiversality where different universality classes describe the same normal-superradiant phase transition, with the dynamical critical exponent zν changing from 1 to 1/2 along a special line.
AI.conclusion: The chiral Dicke model provides a powerful platform for realizing novel quantum phases and multiversal critical phenomena in light-matter coupled systems.

title: Deterministic generation of grid states with programmable nonlinear bosonic circuits
summary: Bosonic quantum error correction enables hardware-efficient protection of quantum information by encoding logical qubits in harmonic oscillators. Bosonic grid states, such as Gottesman-Kitaev-Preskill (GKP) states, are particularly promising due to their potential to correct small displacements and boson loss. However, their generation remains challenging, typically relying on probabilistic protocols or auxiliary qubit systems. Here, we propose deterministic protocols for generating bosonic grid states using programmable nonlinear bosonic circuits composed solely of squeezing, displacement, and Kerr operations. We show that aiming to enforce GKP symmetries in the output of these circuits yields states with competitive performance with respect to current realizations, but whose quality saturates with increasing circuit depth due to imperfect symmetry restoration. Instead, we find that these bosonic circuits naturally give rise to a distinct class of states, that we label as phased-comb states, which are unitarily related to standard grid states but feature an intrinsic phase structure. We demonstrate that these states define a scalable bosonic quantum error-correcting code with near-optimal performance under boson loss comparable to that of approximate GKP states. We further analyze their logical operations and show how to implement a universal gate set for them. Our results establish programmable nonlinear bosonic circuits as a viable route towards the generation of scalable bosonic quantum error-correcting states beyond standard GKP encodings.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes deterministic protocols for generating bosonic grid states using programmable nonlinear bosonic circuits (squeezing, displacement, Kerr operations), introducing a new class of "phased-comb" states that outperform standard GKP states in error correction under boson loss and support universal gate sets.
AI.motivation: Bosonic quantum error correction is hardware-efficient but relies on challenging-to-generate grid states like GKP states, which typically require probabilistic protocols or auxiliary qubit systems.
AI.method: The authors design deterministic protocols using programmable nonlinear bosonic circuits (squeezing, displacement, Kerr operations). They explore two approaches: enforcing GKP symmetries in circuit outputs, and a naturally emerging class called phased-comb states unitarily related to standard grid states but with an intrinsic phase structure.
AI.result: GKP-symmetry-enforced states show competitive but saturating quality with depth. Phased-comb states define a scalable bosonic QEC code with near-optimal performance under boson loss comparable to approximate GKP states, with a universal gate set demonstrated.
AI.conclusion: Programmable nonlinear bosonic circuits offer a viable deterministic route for generating scalable bosonic quantum error-correcting states beyond standard GKP encodings.

title: Replay-buffer engineering for noise-robust quantum circuit optimization
comment: Comments are warmly welcomed. 9 page main content, 17 page appendix
summary: Deep reinforcement learning (RL) for quantum circuit optimization faces three fundamental bottlenecks: replay buffers that ignore the reliability of temporal-difference (TD) targets, curriculum-based architecture search that triggers a full quantum-classical evaluation at every environment step, and the routine discard of noiseless trajectories when retraining under hardware noise. We address all three by treating the replay buffer as a primary algorithmic lever for quantum optimization. We introduce ReaPER$+$, an annealed replay rule that transitions from TD error-driven prioritization early in training to reliability-aware sampling as value estimates mature, achieving $4-32\times$ gains in sample efficiency over fixed PER, ReaPER, and uniform replay while consistently discovering more compact circuits across quantum compilation and QAS benchmarks; validation on LunarLander-v3 confirms the principle is domain-agnostic. Furthermore we eliminate the quantum-classical evaluation bottleneck in curriculum RL by introducing OptCRLQAS which amortizes expensive evaluations over multiple architectural edits, cutting wall-clock time per episode by up to $67.5\%$ on a 12-qubit optimization problem without degrading solution quality. Finally we introduce a lightweight replay-buffer transfer scheme that warm-starts noisy-setting learning by reusing noiseless trajectories, without network-weight transfer or $ε$-greedy pretraining. This reduces steps to chemical accuracy by up to $85-90\%$ and final energy error by up to $90\%$ over from-scratch baselines on 6-, 8-, and 12-qubit molecular tasks. Together, these results establish that experience storage, sampling, and transfer are decisive levers for scalable, noise-robust quantum circuit optimization.

AI.tldr: ReaPER+, OptCRLQAS, and a replay-buffer transfer scheme are proposed to address three bottlenecks in deep RL for quantum circuit optimization: unreliable TD targets, expensive curriculum evaluations, and discarded noiseless trajectories. The methods achieve 4-32× sample efficiency gains, up to 67.5% wall-clock reduction, and 85-90% fewer steps to chemical accuracy.
AI.motivation: Deep RL for quantum circuit optimization suffers from three bottlenecks: (1) replay buffers ignore TD target reliability, (2) curriculum-based architecture search requires a full quantum-classical evaluation at every step, and (3) noiseless trajectories are discarded when retraining under hardware noise.
AI.method: (1) ReaPER+: an annealed replay rule transitioning from TD-error prioritization to reliability-aware sampling. (2) OptCRLQAS: amortizes expensive quantum-classical evaluations over multiple architectural edits. (3) Lightweight replay-buffer transfer: warm-starts noisy training by reusing noiseless trajectories without weight transfer or ε-greedy pretraining.
AI.result: ReaPER+ achieves 4-32× sample efficiency gains over baselines and finds more compact circuits. OptCRLQAS cuts wall-clock time per episode by up to 67.5% on a 12-qubit problem. Replay-buffer transfer reduces steps to chemical accuracy by 85-90% and final energy error by up to 90% on 6-, 8-, and 12-qubit molecular tasks.
