AI.conclusion: Experience storage, sampling, and transfer are decisive levers for scalable, noise-robust quantum circuit optimization. The ReaPER+ principle is also validated to be domain-agnostic on LunarLander-v3.

title: High-performance cellular automaton decoders for quantum repetition and toric code
summary: Execution of quantum algorithms on large-scale quantum computers will require extremely low logical error rates, which necessitates the development of scalable decoding architectures. Local decoders are promising candidates for this task, as they avoid the communication and data processing bottlenecks inherent in global decoding strategies. Cellular automaton (CA) decoders represent a distinct class of local decoders, offering a path toward the low-latency, real-time decoding required for practical applications. In this work, we present SCALA (Signaling CA with Local Attraction), a novel non-hierarchical cellular automaton decoder for quantum repetition and toric codes. By evaluating SCALA alongside the hierarchical CA decoder proposed by Harrington, we provide a direct comparison between non-hierarchical and renormalization-group-style local decoding strategies. We characterize SCALA across three key metrics: Performance, scalability, and robustness. Our results show that SCALA achieves a code-capacity threshold of approximately $p_c\approx 7.5\%$ and provides strong sub-threshold scaling of about $p_L\propto p^{d/4}$ on the toric code. In terms of scalability, our non-hierarchical design ensures that the local computational resources remain independent of system size, yielding a modular local architecture suitable for hardware implementation. Finally, SCALA demonstrates strong robustness to qubit measurement errors and noise within the decoder itself, a critical advantage for real-time decoding on noisy hardware. Our results establish SCALA as a high-performance, scalable, and robust local decoder for scalable quantum error correction.

AI.tldr: SCALA is a novel non-hierarchical cellular automaton decoder for quantum repetition and toric codes, achieving ~7.5% code-capacity threshold, strong sub-threshold scaling (p_L ∝ p^{d/4}), modular and size-independent local architecture, and robustness to measurement errors and decoder noise.
AI.motivation: Scalable quantum computing requires extremely low logical error rates, which demands scalable decoding architectures. Local decoders avoid communication/data bottlenecks inherent in global decoding, and CA decoders offer low-latency real-time decoding. This work introduces SCALA as a non-hierarchical alternative to existing hierarchical CA decoders like Harrington's.
AI.method: The authors design SCALA (Signaling CA with Local Attraction), a non-hierarchical cellular automaton decoder. They evaluate it alongside Harrington's hierarchical CA decoder across three metrics: performance (threshold and sub-threshold scaling), scalability (local resource independence from system size), and robustness (tolerance to measurement errors and decoder noise).
AI.result: SCALA achieves ~7.5% code-capacity threshold on the toric code with sub-threshold scaling p_L ∝ p^{d/4}. The non-hierarchical design ensures local computational resources are independent of system size, yielding a modular architecture. SCALA also shows strong robustness to qubit measurement errors and internal decoder noise.
AI.conclusion: SCALA is established as a high-performance, scalable, and robust local decoder for scalable quantum error correction, suitable for hardware implementation in real-time decoding scenarios.

title: Enhancing Coherence of Spin Centers in p-n Diodes via Optimization Algorithms
summary: Solid-state spin defects hold great promise as building blocks for various quantum technologies. Embedding spin centers in $p$-$n$ diodes under reverse bias has proved to be a powerful strategy to narrow the optical linewidth and increase spin coherence, while also enabling control of the photoluminescence wavelength via Stark shift. Given the multitude of parameters influencing spin centers in diodes (e.g., doping densities and profiles, temperature, bias voltage, spin center position), a question that has not yet been answered is: which set of these design parameters maximizes spin center coherence? In this work, we address this question by developing a scaled gradient descent optimization algorithm that minimizes the optical linewidth of spin centers by combining the numerical solution of a diode's Poisson equation with calculated charge noise from the non-depleted regions. Our optimization is performed for both single- and multiple-parameter cases for divacancies in SiC $p$-$i$-$n$ diodes, including reverse-bias voltage, doping density and profile, and diode total length. Importantly, the optimization is subject to realistic physical constraints, such as small operating bias voltages, avoidance of the dielectric breakdown regime and physical thresholds for doping density. Additionally, due to the leakage current at reverse bias voltages, we develop a new formalism to investigate its influence on coherence. We show that the corresponding noise can be mitigated by implanting spin defects away from the diode's surfaces. Our work provides guidance on experimentally relevant diodes for hosting spin centers with the narrowest optical linewidths and longest coherence times.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a scaled gradient descent optimization algorithm to minimize optical linewidth (maximize coherence) of spin centers in p-n diodes, applied to divacancies in SiC p-i-n diodes, considering realistic physical constraints and leakage current noise.
AI.motivation: To identify the optimal set of design parameters (doping densities/profiles, temperature, bias voltage, spin center position) that maximizes spin center coherence in diodes, a question not previously answered despite the known benefits of embedding spin centers in reverse-biased p-n junctions.
AI.method: A scaled gradient descent optimization algorithm combining numerical solution of a diode's Poisson equation with calculated charge noise from non-depleted regions, applied to single- and multiple-parameter cases for divacancies in SiC p-i-n diodes, with constraints on bias voltage, dielectric breakdown, and doping density; plus a new formalism for leakage current noise.
AI.result: The optimization yields optimal parameter sets for narrowest optical linewidth; leakage current noise can be mitigated by implanting spin defects away from diode surfaces.
AI.conclusion: The work provides experimentally relevant guidance for designing diodes that host spin centers with the narrowest optical linewidths and longest coherence times.

title: Loss-biased fault-tolerant quantum error correction
summary: We investigate the limits of quantum error correction (QEC) in neutral-atom processors approaching high-fidelity gates and fast cycle times. We show that shorter QEC cycles amplify platform-specific errors, notably Rydberg excitation hopping, and hinder decay of residual Rydberg population, leading to non-Markovian correlated errors that degrade logical performance. To address this, we introduce loss biasing, where spurious Rydberg excitations are rapidly converted into atom loss via mid-circuit ionization, transforming errors into erasure-like noise and suppressing their propagation. Loss biasing restores the fault-tolerant logical error scaling for intra-cycle Pauli errors; furthermore, we argue that when supported with loss-aware decoding, it can achieve the optimal scaling of erasures while enabling shorter QEC cycles with reduced hardware overhead. We outline an implementation using fast autoionization in alkaline-earth(-like) atoms, establishing loss biasing as a practical route toward fault-tolerant quantum computing with sub-millisecond QEC cycles.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates quantum error correction (QEC) limits in neutral-atom processors with high-fidelity gates and fast cycle times, showing that shorter QEC cycles amplify platform-specific errors like Rydberg excitation hopping and residual Rydberg population decay, causing non-Markovian correlated errors. The authors propose "loss biasing"—converting spurious Rydberg excitations into atom loss via mid-circuit ionization—to transform errors into erasure-like noise and restore fault-tolerant logical error scaling, potentially achieving optimal erasure scaling with loss-aware decoding and enabling sub-millisecond QEC cycles.
AI.motivation: To understand the fundamental limits of quantum error correction in neutral-atom processors as they approach high-fidelity gates and fast cycle times, and to address the unique platform-specific error mechanisms (Rydberg hopping, residual population decay) that degrade logical performance in short QEC cycles.
AI.method: The paper introduces "loss biasing," a technique where spurious Rydberg excitations are rapidly converted into atom loss via mid-circuit ionization, transforming these errors into erasure-like noise. This is combined with loss-aware decoding to suppress error propagation and restore fault-tolerant scaling.
AI.result: Loss biasing restores the fault-tolerant logical error scaling for intra-cycle Pauli errors and, when supported with loss-aware decoding, can achieve the optimal scaling of erasures while enabling shorter QEC cycles with reduced hardware overhead.
AI.conclusion: Loss biasing, implementable via fast autoionization in alkaline-earth(-like) atoms, provides a practical route toward fault-tolerant quantum computing with sub-millisecond QEC cycles in neutral-atom processors.

title: A Universal Quantum Information Preserving Photonic Switch for Scalable Quantum Networks
summary: Quantum networks are a keystone of the quantum internet. However, existing implementations remain largely confined to static point-to-point links due to the absence of a switching paradigm capable of dynamically routing fragile quantum entanglement without introducing decoherence. Here, we propose the Universal Quantum Switch, a foundational building block allowing on-demand, non-blocking, and encoding-agnostic routing of quantum information, as well as seamless modality conversion between disparate quantum platforms. We develop a prototype in thin-film lithium niobate and experimentally demonstrate robust switching with $\le 4\%$ decoherence via thermo-optic modulation and high-speed electro-optic switching of arbitrary entangled states at 1 MHz. Moreover, we show that our platform can support reconfiguration speeds up to 1 GHz. To our knowledge, this work represents the first demonstration of multi-node dynamic entanglement distribution at these speeds. Complementing these experimental results, we project the architecture's scalability, showing dimension-independent decoherence, and provide a scalable, interoperable building block for heterogeneous quantum network fabrics.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes and demonstrates a Universal Quantum Switch for dynamic, non-blocking, encoding-agnostic routing of quantum entanglement with low decoherence (≤4%), achieving up to 1 GHz reconfiguration speeds, enabling scalable quantum networks beyond static point-to-point links.
AI.motivation: Existing quantum networks are limited to static point-to-point links due to the lack of a switching paradigm capable of dynamically routing fragile quantum entanglement without introducing decoherence.
AI.method: The authors develop a Universal Quantum Switch in thin-film lithium niobate, employing thermo-optic modulation and high-speed electro-optic switching to route arbitrary entangled states at 1 MHz, with projected reconfiguration speeds up to 1 GHz.
AI.result: Robust switching with ≤4% decoherence was experimentally demonstrated, alongside the first multi-node dynamic entanglement distribution at these speeds. Scalability analysis shows dimension-independent decoherence.
AI.conclusion: The Universal Quantum Switch serves as a scalable, interoperable building block for heterogeneous quantum network fabrics, enabling the quantum internet beyond static links.

title: Efficient Classical Simulation of Heuristic Peaked Quantum Circuits
summary: Peaked quantum circuits, whose output distribution is sharply concentrated on a single bitstring, have emerged as a promising candidate for verifiable quantum advantage, as the correctness of the quantum output can be checked by simply comparing against the known peak. Recent work by Gharibyan et al. arXiv:2510.25838 claimed heuristic quantum advantage using peaked circuits executed on Quantinuum's 56-qubit H2 processor. These peaked circuits concentrate their output on a single hidden bitstring by training a shallow simulable circuit variationally and inserting an obfuscated permutation to increase the depth to a level that makes classical simulation intractable, with estimated runtimes of years for the largest instances. We show that these circuits can be efficiently simulated classically. We describe a method that efficiently performs a full tensor network contraction, allowing near-exact sampling and extraction of the peaked bitstring. The method exploits the mirrored structure of the circuit and iteratively cancels both halves into a Matrix Product Operator (MPO), and avoids the obfuscated permutation by greedily reducing the MPO bond dimension through a process we call unswapping. The method can fully contract and extract the peak of the largest circuit in approximately one hour on a single GPU, around half the time it took to run on the quantum hardware.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that peaked quantum circuits claimed to demonstrate verifiable quantum advantage can actually be efficiently simulated classically using tensor network contraction, Matrix Product Operators, and an "unswapping" technique, completing the largest instance in about one hour on a single GPU—faster than the quantum hardware run time.
AI.motivation: Peaked quantum circuits have been proposed as candidates for verifiable quantum advantage, with recent work claiming heuristic advantage on a 56-qubit Quantinuum H2 processor. The question is whether these circuits genuinely resist classical simulation.
AI.method: The method performs full tensor network contraction by exploiting the mirrored circuit structure, iteratively canceling both halves into a Matrix Product Operator (MPO), and using a greedy "unswapping" process to reduce MPO bond dimension while avoiding the obfuscated permutation.
AI.result: The largest circuit can be fully contracted and the peak bitstring extracted in approximately one hour on a single GPU, roughly half the time required to run on the quantum hardware.
AI.conclusion: The claimed quantum advantage from peaked circuits is not valid, as classical simulation is not only possible but more efficient than the quantum execution itself.

title: Dual-use quantum hardware for quantum resource generation and energy storage
summary: Quantum resources such as entanglement form the backbone of quantum technologies and their efficient generation is a central objective of modern quantum platforms. Independently, quantum batteries have emerged as nanoscale devices that utilize collective quantum effects to store energy with a charging advantage over classical strategies. Here, we show that these two pursuits can co-exist: protocols for fast generation of resourceful quantum states can simultaneously charge a quantum battery with a collective advantage, and conversely, a quantum battery protocol with a charging advantage can produce resource-rich states. Using this connection, we propose an integrated hardware protocol on superconducting circuits in which each experimental run can interchangeably accomplish either quantum battery charging, or quantum sensing through generation of metrologically useful states. Our results establish that quantum resources and stored energy are distinct yet co-producable quantities, opening the door to modular quantum architectures that dynamically switch between sensing and energy-storage functions, thereby producing additional functionalities without extra hardware cost.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that fast generation of quantum resource states and quantum battery charging can be mutually beneficial, and proposes a superconducting circuit protocol that enables switching between quantum sensing and energy storage without extra hardware.
AI.motivation: To demonstrate that the generation of quantum resources (like entanglement) and quantum battery charging with collective advantage are compatible pursuits, and to propose a unified hardware protocol that can dynamically switch between sensing and energy-storage functions.
AI.method: The authors establish a connection between protocols for fast generation of resourceful quantum states and quantum battery protocols with charging advantages. They then design an integrated hardware protocol on superconducting circuits where each experimental run can interchangeably perform quantum battery charging or quantum sensing via metrologically useful state generation.
AI.result: Quantum resources and stored energy are distinct yet co-producible quantities. The proposed protocol allows a single superconducting circuit platform to switch between sensing and energy-storage functionalities without extra hardware costs.
AI.conclusion: This work opens the door to modular quantum architectures that dynamically switch between quantum sensing and energy storage, providing additional functionalities without requiring additional hardware overhead.

title: Subsystem-Resolved Spectral Theory for Quantum Many-Body Hamiltonians
summary: We study spectral properties of quantum many-body Hamiltonians through a subsystem-based framework. Given a Hamiltonian of the form $H = \sum_{X \subseteq Λ} Φ(X)$ acting on a tensor product Hilbert space, we associate to each subset $S \subseteq Λ$ a subsystem Hamiltonian $H_S$ and its spectrum $\mathcal{S}(S) = σ(H_S)$. This produces a family of spectra indexed by subsystems, allowing spectral data to be organized according to interaction structure. We show that subsystem Hamiltonians admit local approximations: $H_S$ can be approximated by operators supported on finite neighborhoods with an error bounded by $\|H_S - H_{S,r}\| \le |S| e^{-μr} \|Φ\|_μ$. As a consequence, subsystem spectra are stable under truncation in the sense that $d_H(\mathcal{S}(S), σ(H_{S,r})) \le |S| e^{-μr} \|Φ\|_μ.$ We then prove that for disjoint subsets $S_1, S_2 \subseteq Λ$, the subsystem spectrum is approximately additive: $d_H\big(\mathcal{S}(S_1 \cup S_2), \mathcal{S}(S_1) + \mathcal{S}(S_2)\big) \le (|S_1| + |S_2|) e^{-μD} \|Φ\|_μ,$ where $D = d(S_1, S_2)$. In the finite-range case, this relation becomes exact. The results show that spectral properties reflect the locality of interactions not only at the level of operators, but also at the level of spectra. The framework provides a way to study many-body systems in which interaction geometry directly shapes spectral behavior.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a subsystem-based spectral framework for quantum many-body Hamiltonians, proving that subsystem spectra admit local approximations with exponential error bounds and are approximately additive for disjoint subsystems, with exact additivity in the finite-range case.
AI.motivation: To understand how local interaction structure shapes spectral properties of quantum many-body Hamiltonians, moving beyond operator-level locality to spectral-level organization.
AI.method: Associating subsystem Hamiltonians $H_S$ to subsets $S \subseteq \Lambda$; proving exponential error bounds for local approximations $H_{S,r}$; establishing approximate additivity of spectra for disjoint subsystems via Hausdorff distance $d_H$.
AI.result: (1) $\|H_S - H_{S,r}\| \le |S| e^{-\mu r} \|\Phi\|_\mu$; (2) $d_H(\mathcal{S}(S), \sigma(H_{S,r})) \le |S| e^{-\mu r} \|\Phi\|_\mu$; (3) $d_H(\mathcal{S}(S_1 \cup S_2), \mathcal{S}(S_1) + \mathcal{S}(S_2)) \le (|S_1|+|S_2|) e^{-\mu D} \|\Phi\|_\mu$; exact additivity for finite-range interactions.
AI.conclusion: Spectral properties reflect interaction locality, providing a framework where interaction geometry directly shapes spectral behavior in many-body systems.
